name: Free Energy Minimisation

source.author: Karl Friston
source.paper: The free-energy principle: a unified brain theory?

biological_basis: The brain maintains an internal generative model of the world and continuously minimises variational free energy — a bound on surprise — by updating beliefs about hidden causes or by acting to change sensory input.
core_claim: All adaptive biological systems minimise free energy (surprise) through perception (belief updating) and action (changing the world to match predictions). Cognition is fundamentally predictive, not reactive.

implementable_mechanism.description: Maintain a generative model that predicts sensory states. Compute prediction error as mismatch between predicted and observed states. Update model parameters to reduce error; optionally select actions that reduce expected future error.
implementable_mechanism.pseudocode: prediction = model.predict(prior); error = observation - prediction; beliefs = beliefs + lr * gradient(error); action = argmin_a expected_future_error(a)

notes: Central unifying objective for EIDOS. PredictionEngine minimises surprise as its core loop.

name: Active Inference

source.author: Karl Friston, Thomas FitzGerald, Francesco Rigoli, Philipp Schwartenbeck, Giovanni Pezzulo

biological_basis: Perception and action are two sides of the same inference process: the agent selects actions that minimise expected free energy by fulfilling prior predictions about preferred states while updating beliefs from sensory evidence.
core_claim: Action is not separate from perception — acting is a way of sampling the world to confirm predictions. Expected free energy combines ambiguity reduction and pragmatic value (goal fulfilment).

implementable_mechanism.description: At each timestep, compute expected free energy for candidate actions as sum of epistemic value (information gain) and pragmatic value (goal proximity). Select action minimising EFE; update beliefs from resulting observations.
implementable_mechanism.pseudocode: for a in actions: EFE[a] = epistemic(a) + pragmatic(a); action = argmin(EFE); observe = world.step(action); beliefs = bayesian_update(beliefs, observe)

notes: Maps to EIDOS goal-directed attention (pragmatic) combined with surprise-driven exploration (epistemic).

name: Hierarchical Predictive Coding

source.author: Rajesh P.N. Rao, Dana H. Ballard
source.paper: Predictive coding in the visual cortex: a functional interpretation of some extra-classical receptive-field effects

biological_basis: Cortical hierarchy uses top-down predictions and bottom-up prediction errors. Higher areas predict activity in lower areas; only errors propagate upward. Extra-classical receptive field effects arise from contextual predictions.
core_claim: Visual cortex implements a hierarchical predictive coding scheme where each level predicts the representation at the level below, and learning reduces prediction error via Hebbian-like updates.

implementable_mechanism.description: Two or more layers: lower layer receives input; higher layer predicts lower-layer activity. Error = input - prediction propagates up; predictions propagate down. Weights updated to minimise squared error at each layer.
implementable_mechanism.pseudocode: L1_pred = W1 @ input + b1; L1_err = input - L1_pred; L2_pred = W2 @ context + b2; L2_err = L1_pred - L2_pred; update W1, W2 via SGD on errors

notes: Direct blueprint for PredictionEngine two-layer hierarchy in EIDOS.
